title: Gampi and mitsumata sheet forming
subtitle: Pulling translucent sheets from the two finest Japanese washi fibres
excerpt: Gampi and mitsumata fibres produce the most refined washi: thin, translucent sheets with a natural sheen and long-fibre structure that holds a dip-pen line without any sizing. Both fibres are slower to process than kozo and require more careful vat preparation, but the resulting sheets are among the most beautiful in the handmade-paper tradition.
sourceNotes: Tim Barrett, Japanese Papermaking: Traditions, Tools, and Techniques (1983, Weatherhill), chapter on gampi and mitsumata fibres. The nagashizuki (flow technique) adaptation for home studio use follows Barrett's descriptions for Western mould-and-deckle pulling.

content.text: are used in the Japanese nagashizuki technique with

to slow drainage. In a home studio the standard Western float-and-scoop works fine with these fibres. The sheets come out less refined than traditional Japanese paper. They are still far finer than kozo or cotton-linter paper.

items.qty: 30 g, gampi fibre is harder to source than kozo; sheets of gampi washi can be repulped effectively
items.name: Gampi washi sheets (purchased as finished paper, to be repulped) or dried gampi fibre strips
Mitsumata washi (optional, for a blended sheet)

attrs.heading: Materials for one A5 session (4-6 sheets)

content.text: Soak torn pieces of gampi paper or dried fibre in cool water for 20 minutes. Blend in small batches with 500 ml water for only 15-20 seconds per batch, gampi fibres shorten significantly when over-blended, losing the length that gives the sheet its sheen and strength. The blended slurry should still contain some visibly longer fibres. Over-blending is the most common error with these fine fibres.
Dilute the vat and pull sheets

Gampi and mitsumata need a more dilute vat than kozo, roughly 0.5% fibre. Add the blended pulp to the vat and top up with water until the surface looks almost completely clear. Pull sheets with a gentle scooping motion, lifting the mould slowly and levelling it as it comes out of the water. The thin slurry forms thin sheets; pulling two passes (pouring a ladleful over the mould after the initial pull, then draining) gives a slightly heavier sheet.

Gampi and mitsumata sheets are very fragile when wet: the natural self-sizing of gampi makes the wet sheet slightly slippery, and mitsumata sheets tear easily at the couch. Couch onto a smooth plastic sheet rather than a felt, the sheet peels off plastic more cleanly once partly dry. Do not press under heavy weight; allow to dry freely at room temperature. Once dry, gampi paper has a translucent, parchment-like quality that is characteristic of the fibre.
